data.title: Goldman Sachs: Thank God The Apple Saga Is Over
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my last article on Goldman Sachs (GS), I analyzed the company’s third-quarter results and evaluated whether the company is benefiting from a favorable macro backdrop regarding both dealmaking and wealth management. I had a HOLD rating on the stock.

Since the article was published in October 2025, the stock has gained 22.3%, significantly outperforming the S&P 500, which gained 5.2% during the same period.

GS reported its Q4 numbers nearly two weeks ago, and while the quarter was largely mixed, the biggest development was that the bank was finally able to finalize the sale of its Apple credit card business.

In this article, I analyze the road ahead for GS post the Apple saga and also evaluate whether the tailwinds that propelled the bank to new heights in 2025 still remain going ahead and how GS can capitalize on these tailwinds if they do exist.

Fourth Quarter Highlights

GS had a mixed quarter to finish the year, with a beat on the bottom line but a miss on the top line. More specifically, Q4 revenues came in at $13.45 billion, down 2.99% y/y and missing street estimates by $395.3 million. Diluted EPS came in at $14.01, up 17% y/y and significantly beating analyst estimates by $2.25. The book value per share saw a 1.1% jump y/y and now stands at $357.60.

The bank’s annualized ROE came in at 16%, expanding by 1.8% sequentially and by 1.4% on a y/y basis. Finally, GS boosted its quarterly dividend by 12.5% to $4.50, which represents a forward yield of 1.93%.

Apple Card Exit Comes at an Opportune Time for GS’s AWM Business

GS’s prominence when it comes to dealmaking isn’t going away any time soon, as evidenced by the continued growth seen in Q4. More specifically, the Global Banking & Markets segment saw revenues grow 22.4% y/y and 2.4% sequentially. The y/y growth, in particular, was impressive, as it was the third highest growth in the last 13 quarters. And with the market environment continuing to be ripe for dealmaking, especially with debt-fueled deals also making a comeback, I expect the Global Banking & Markets segment’s growth to keep accelerating in the coming months. Moreover, given that the market environment is expected to remain volatile, GS’s trading revenues should also continue their upward trajectory, subsequently boosting the Global Banking & Markets segment.

While GS’s dealmaking prowess is largely expected to be maintained in the coming quarters, the bank’s AWM business is at an inflection point, in my view. The segment has the potential to be a strong catalyst for the overall growth. This is because, in my opinion, with the sale of AAPL’s credit card business finally secured, GS’s strategic focus can finally pivot towards enhancing the AWM business, a point that the management repeatedly acknowledged during the Q4 earnings call.

The sale of AAPL’s credit card business puts an end to the tumultuous period for GS, where it embarked on a strategy to boost its consumer lending business. Now that the final nail in the coffin has been hammered on the flawed strategy, GS, in my view, has the perfect opportunity to convert its already strong AWM business into a strong long-term catalyst.

The sale also comes at a perfect time for GS, as it is largely expected to be immune now from the threat of the potential interest-rate cap proposed by the Trump administration. For instance, the sale releases nearly $2.5 billion in loan loss reserves for GS and eliminates the capital drag of holding unsecured consumer loans as well as the distractions of running a business that was never the strength of GS. It also allows GS to fully focus on its Wall Street and the HNWI segments, areas that GS is renowned for.

Furthermore, we are currently going through a private credit supercycle. In my opinion, the unique position that GS finds itself in, whereby it can direct its investment banking clients to its AWM private credit funds, offers it an advantage over pure-play asset managers or money center banks such as (C). Furthermore, banks such as (JPM) are only beginning to show interest in taking advantage of the private credit boom, which demonstrates that not only does GS have a head start, but also that the private credit supercycle is here to stay.

Finally, from a macro perspective, the [AWM] industry is poised to grow significantly in the coming years. More specifically, according to a recent report from PwC, the global Assets Under Management [AUM] are projected to grow to $200 trillion by 2030. The report highlights that this growth presents an opportunity to capture as much as $230 billion of new revenue, with strong demand seen in private markets. Given that GS excels in deep origination capabilities, this trend is expected to favor GS immensely. The bank is strong in the alternatives and active management space, which commands higher fees relative to passive index funds. As such, the strong growth trajectory of the AWM industry should also lead to higher fee growth for GS.

The sale of AAPL’s credit card business enables GS to move from a bank with a confused identity as a result of a lack of a focused strategy to one that not only sticks to its strengths [Investment Banking] but also takes advantage of its strengths to bolster a segment that is likely to be a growth compounder given the tailwinds.

Valuation

Forward P/B Approach

Price Target

$998.00

Projected Forward P/B Multiple

2.56x

Projected 2026 Book Value Per Share

$389.72

Source: Calculations based on Author’s Model

I deployed a fundamentals-driven valuation approach, validated by ridge regression machine learning, to predict the Book Value Per Share [BVPS] of GS at the end of 2026. More specifically, the model was built on the following fundamental relationship between Return on Equity [ROE] and growth in book value:

The retention ratio is the proportion of earnings retained after making the necessary payouts [calculated as 1 – payout ratio].

Ridge Regression was selected for validation after evaluating multiple algorithms, including XGBoost, Gradient Boosting, and ElasticNet. The algorithm handled the issue of multicollinearity among financial features better, and its built-in L2 regularization prevented overfitting, a critical requirement given that book value time series models are prone to autocorrelation. More information on Ridge Regression can be found here.

The model employed nine features (predictive variables), which captured the economic drivers of book value growth. This is a more robust method of forecasting when compared to methods that heavily rely on lagged BVPS values, which merely extrapolate recent trends. The features included Rolling 4-quarter ROE, Current Quarter ROE, ROE-implied Growth, Average Book Value Accretion over the last four quarters, Average EPS growth over the last four quarters, debt-to-equity, change in share count, ROE trend, and Seasonality.

The model was trained using 96 quarterly observations from Q1-2002 to Q4-2025. Financial Modelling Prep [FMP] Premium API was used to obtain the observations. The model had a mean absolute error [MAE] of 1.82% during the training phase and an MAE of 1.73% during the testing phase. For instance, GS’s actual BVPS for the most recent quarter was $357.60, and the predicted BVPS from the model was $352.61, which represents an error of just 1.4%. The model was also well-calibrated as it achieved a near-zero systematic error.

A scenario analysis was also conducted given the uncertain macro environment that investors find themselves in. The assumptions of the three scenarios, along with the model’s Q4 2026 BVPS predictions and the corresponding price targets under each scenario, are shown in the table below:

Each scenario was then assigned probabilities based on the current macro outlook, industry outlook, and GS-specific factors:

Scenario

Probability (%)

Price Target

Probability x Price Target

Conservative

$839.00

$210.00

Base Case

50%

$998.00

$499.00

Optimistic

$289.00

Weighted Average

$998.00

The weighted average price target implies an upside of about 8% from current levels. When one combines a dividend yield of about 2%, the total return potential from current levels is about 10%. GS also has incredibly strong momentum, as evidenced by the overall A+ grade and a short-term momentum grade of A on Seeking Alpha’s Momentum scale. Taking things together, I am upgrading my rating on GS from a HOLD to a BUY.

Risk Factors

The key risk factors to my bull case were summarized in the Conservative scenario in the previous section. In addition to these factors, any exogenous shock to the private credit market could also have an adverse effect on GS’s AWM business, subsequently having an adverse impact on the bank’s [ROE].

Concluding Thoughts

GS may have had a mixed quarter, but the bank remains well-positioned to capitalize on the macro tailwinds, especially in the capital markets. With both the M&A and IPO markets set to continue their momentum, and continued elevated volatility expected to remain a tailwind for the bank’s trading division, the overall growth trajectory for GS remains intact.

To me, however, the biggest blessing for GS investors would be the end of the torturous saga of the bank’s consumer lending business now that the sale of AAPL’s credit card business is finalised. GS can finally move on from the debacle and focus on its key strengths, with the renewed focus allowing the bank to capitalize on the tailwinds.

From a valuation perspective, my model suggests an upside of about 10% from current levels. Furthermore, the stock also has exceptional momentum. Considering these factors, I have upgraded my rating on the stock to BUY from a HOLD.

Overall, 2025 was a blockbuster year for GS. With the consumer lending saga behind it, 2026 has the potential to be an even bigger year, in my opinion.

data.title: TriplePoint Venture: I Tried To Plot A Bull Case, But I Failed
data.description:
February, 2024, I wrote my first piece on TriplePoint Venture Growth (NYSE:TPVG) titled: TriplePoint Venture Growth: Not Worth Chasing Yield Here.

My decision back then to stay away from TPVG was mostly linked to venture-stage funding and not that many registered equity gains, which, in my view, was the key to justify above average risk taking. On top of that, TPVG had one of the highest portfolio yields and quite aggressive debt load - gross leverage of ~1.75x (usually, anything that exceeds 1.2x is already considered risky).

This is how the things have evolved since that article:

TPVG has not only underperformed the sector (BIZD), but also failed to participate in the venture-related momentum that Hercules Capital, Inc. (NYSE:HTGC) and Trinity Capital (TRIN) had enjoyed (other two VC-focused BDCs).

Just as I predicted, during the process TPVG was forced to adjust the dividend two times in order to make the numbers work - i.e., reduce the reliance on debt to fund the part of dividend that was not covered by net interest income generation.

Now, currently after all of this mess, it seems that things have, at least on the paper (price chart) started to stabilize for TPVG. In the past 3-month period, TPVG has registered ~18% in total returns, while offering ~14% dividend.

On a P/NAV basis, TPVG remains as one of the cheapest BDCs in the sector with the discount to NAV standing at 26% (the sector average is 13%).

The M&A markets are also opening up and coming back with a vengeance, which is a major tailwind for VC-focused capital allocators like TPVG and, of course, TRIN and HTGC that have been also having fun lately.

With this backdrop in mind, let me share my thoughts and update my thesis on TPVG, which based on the recent signs so far definitely looks like an interesting case (at least worth taking a look at).

Thesis review

Let me say this right out of the gate that TPVG, as a BDC or any investment, has 'broken a promise' for me of not delivering unpleasant news in the form of dividend cuts that have stemmed from poor risk management.

Irrespective of what the following data (or my text) indicates below, I would not include TPVG in my core BDC portfolio bucket, which consists of durable, defensive and high-margin of safety BDCs, where I have a strong conviction that my dividend income will not get disrupted by credit risk materialization (i.e., I'm not ruling out dividend normalizations that come from lower base rates, which is a completely organic process in the private credit space).

However, where I could see TPVG playing a role in my overall portfolio is in the tactical BDC portfolio bucket, which is much smaller and comprised of high risk, high return bets. The objective of this bucket is to diversify the BDC holdings even further and, more importantly, allow me access potentially abnormal returns, where I have reasonably strong case for superior risk-adjusted return plays (high risk, but even higher return, which makes the ratio attractive).

When it comes to TPVG, then the fact that I mentioned above about the discount to NAV is a very solid starting point. Having so depressed valuation opens the door for potentially harvesting significant gains from the multiple recovery. The dividend yield of ~14% also helps.

Yet, for all this to play out, TPVG has to deliver sustainable returns and shield the current dividend from another cut.

Now, if we start with the income side, the last two dividend cuts have indeed helped bring the coverage level into a solid shape. As of Q3, 2025, TPVG had one of the best base dividend coverage levels in the sector - at 113%. From this surplus, TPVG can put some internally generated equity at work to fund the incoming demands for VC loans (e.g., in the last quarter, TPVG had the highest level of debt commitments and fundings since 2022).

Of course, the Q3 report did not include the recent three base rate cuts by the Fed, which is inherently negative to BDC NII per share levels. In TPVG's case, just as for TRIN, the NII profile is actually strongly positioned to withstand the SOFR-driven pressures. See comment from the Management below:

66% of our debt portfolio is floating rate and 46% of those floating rate loans were at their floors as of September 30. Following the 25 basis point Fed rate cut in late October 2025, floating rate loans at their rate floors increased to 52%. As a result, we expect the impact of any further interest rate reductions on our net investment income to be limited, particularly as lower base rates would also reduce interest expense on our floating rate revolving debt.

There will be an impact, but not at such a magnitude that would erode the entire NII surplus. Moreover, the fact that the demand for VC-loan products is so strong, we could even make a case that TPVG would be able to register NII per share growth.

However, now comes the offsetting part to this positive story.

Approximately, 22% of TPVG's total investment income comes from PIK, which is a non-cash item. So, already here we arrive at a conclusion that on a cash-basis TPVG's dividend is undercovered.

While the debt has gone down from what the BDC had in early 2024, it still remains growth-restrictive. The debt to equity is 1.32x, which is not the right level to have when each quarter TPVG has to tap into credit facility to facilitate cash distributions.

What we have to also consider is the fact that TPVG has waived incentive fees, which will not emerge this year as well. Given that TPVG is externally managed BDC, it is quite obvious that this kind of setup is unsustainable (i.e., the Management has to make money). Namely, we should factor in that sooner or later the fees will come back, putting an additional pressure on the dividend.

The only way TPVG can pull the rabbit out of a hat is by monetizing its warrants and equity stakes, which currently carry a fair value of ~$134 million or a bit over today's market cap.

And this is a very subjective and/or hard to predict thing. I will give you two reasons that provide positive indications of that scenario happening and two reasons that work against it.

The positive:

  • The transaction activity is gaining momentum as, among other things, the interest rates have become more supportive for deal making. In this kind of environment, TPVG as an equity holder can indeed succeed.
  • Both warrant and equity portfolio of TPVG have appreciated in value relative to the investment cost, which generally sends a signal that there are some pockets of well-performing cases that could be interesting for the market.

The negative:

  • TPVG's loan investment portfolio is underwater (FV below cost basis), which just confirms that the BDC is dependent on a couple of success stories that sit within its warrant/equity portfolio (e.g., Revolut).
  • The rate of change in non-accrual statistic has again been negative in Q3, 2025, indicating that the struggles (write-downs) are likely still ahead, which might offset the positives that would come from warrant/equity monetizations.

The bottom line

In my view, TPVG is an excellent definition of high risk, high reward case.

If the BDC succeeds in selling part of its warrant/equity portfolio, then the returns could really go ballistic through a P/NAV recovery and continued 14% dividend. In that case, TPVG would be also in a position to use the liquidity to pay down debt or, more importantly, fund new growth opportunities.

If the divestitures do not take place or come in at relatively minor amounts, then I see a slow and steady bleed for TPVG, which would eventually lead to another dividend cut just to avoid 'leverage-funded dividend status'.

When it comes to tactical or high risk, high reward BDC investments is to have the return side more explained by variables that the management can control and the risk side not associated with a process, which is already heading into a dividend cut direction, where the only thing that can stop it is the success of finding a buyer for TPVG equity stakes (and in the warrant case, the portfolio companies themselves deciding to do an IPO or attract fresh equity capital).

In a nutshell, I'm still not going long TPVG.

data.title: Strattec Security: Very Conservative Balance Sheet, But Politics Matter More
data.description:
Strattec Security Corporation (STRT) has long been in the automotive lock business. The company has a few other related products that interest its automotive customers as well. Basically, the major market remains in Detroit Michigan where much of the automotive industry is located. Currently there are several headwinds facing this industry besides its cyclical nature. All of these and likely a few more affect the company.

Last Article

Back when I wrote the last article, things were looking pretty good. Covid was not even a thought on the horizon back in 2016. The company was thinking about making some acquisitions to broaden the product line and perhaps lessen the cyclical nature of the business.

Even back then, the balance sheet was a top priority as this company generally disdains long-term debt and maintains a cash balance.

Currently, that cash balance is roughly $90 million which is huge for a company of this size. But ever since it went public back in 1995, a strong balance sheet has been a goal for as long as I can remember.

For those investors that buy and never sell (and like conservative balance sheets), this company is probably a "must" consideration. It has an outstanding share of the car locks market (and has for a long time).

Arguably, the stock price is probably near a cyclical high point. But for some that again buy and never sell, that may not be a hindrance because management has grown the business over time (although slowly). So even if there is a cyclical downturn next, the next cycle is likely to prove to have higher high stock prices and higher low stock prices.

For that same reason though, some may wait for the next inevitable cyclical downturn before buying and never selling.

In any event, companies, this conservative rarely get into serious trouble. The finances are so solid that all kinds of companies will be financially stressed long before this one is.

Political Headwinds

For the rest of us that either trade or at least claim to buy bargains, this stock is probably in the hold category because some might start a position and then wait for a bargain price to add considerably to the position. Others will read up on the company and study the business while waiting for a bargain price.

But politics unfortunately dominate current quarterly results. The auto industry is particularly sensitive to both political and economic policies. Neither seems to be going in the right direction right now according to many. But that could be bad news for a company that is currently reporting decent results.

Arguing against purchasing right now is the fact that the economic policy is very much against anything I ever learned in economics class. Now one thing about economics is that timing is not part of the theory or any research results. Therefore, as to when trouble will appear, that is entirely another matter compared to pressure building for that trouble.

According to my Samuelson textbook "Economics" it is classic theory that increasing deficits combined with a call for lower interest rates will build inflationary pressures. Just keep in mind that there are two times this went on before and it took years before it showed itself.

Those two examples include the Bush administration from 2001-2008 where 7 years of increasing deficits combined with low interest rates led to 2008. Then there was the first Trump administration where years of increasing deficits starting in 2017 led to some real hard choices during covid and inflation during the recovery years.

In both cases, the building pressure was noted by economists who knew where to look and the situation could have been corrected all along the way. There are various opinions as to when things got critical. But the key here is that inflation did not appear "yesterday" in either case and in fact what happened in 2008 was a collapse rather than inflation. That means that building pressures can express themselves in a number of ways. It makes economics very confusing for the average investor and voter.

Economics depends upon a number of factors beyond our control. Therefore, the prescribed result may take some time to show itself. Until then the building pressures can be either released or abated with minimal damage. Usually that does not happen "because the economy is going great so there is nothing to fix."

Tariffs

The other consideration is tariffs. Tariffs are basically defined as a tax on our citizens on certain imported products. What is relevant here is all the research that led to the United States establishing an income tax rather than relying on tariff income. The conclusions of that research have not changed in decades.

But again, the results of that research lack a time element. Further complicating the picture is a constant changing of the tariff strategy.

In the long-term tariffs are seen as inefficient. Strattec in particular manufactures in Mexico and imports the parts to its automotive customers. Therefore, any tariff policy is very important to this company.

A long-term example is the sugar tariff to benefit our sugar industry. This tariff enables the high fructose corn syrup industry in the Midwest to exist. Eliminating that tariff would cost a whole lot of jobs but would also significantly lower our sugar costs.

For Strattec, potentially avoiding higher product costs from a tariff might mean bringing that manufacturing back to the United States. But there is currently a worker shortage for that type of job (for starters) as has been the case for several decades. Those jobs do not pay as well as they used to (comparatively speaking) and so Americans do not want those jobs. The days of quitting high school and getting a good factory job are basically over.

Even if you get past that economic change, locks made in the United States would cost more than locks made in Mexico (or any other part the company makes in Mexico).

Any increase in costs threatens to bring about an industry recession. Admittedly, it takes enough of those costs to make it apparent to Americans that they need to delay buying a new car. But we may be on the verge of that with the current administration.

Inflation Fighting

The main job of keeping inflation under control remains with the Federal Reserve. The increasing deficits that put pressure on the Federal Reserve to borrow more money may in fact put some inflation fighting pressure on Congress and the President in the future.

But until there is sufficient evidence of that, the main immediate inflation fighter is the Federal Reserve.

Note that while increasing deficits and low interest rates cause inflation pressures to build, the Federal Reserve often has plenty of time to offset those pressures.

However, also note that the administration from 2017-2020 was seen as populist. Populists are liberal fiscal policy makers by definition. That is part of my economics book as well but you can find it in a number of places.

Therefore, it is important to note that Joe Biden, who was wrongly blamed (solely) for the inflation that occurred during his term for all the reasons covered above (including the fact that the Federal Reserve fights inflation), did not appoint members to take office until May 2022. That means that the Federal Reserve appointees in control of the Federal Reserve were all Trump administration appointees at the time inflation began to assert itself.

But again, a populist tends to be a fiscal liberal, therefore inflation pressure should be expected to build when a populist is in office. In the case of the last bout of inflation, the administration in charge at the time had no appointees on the board to influence the inflation fight before it began.

This is going to be important this time around when considering the challenges that a company like Strattec Security might face in the future.

Summary

The Strattec Security earnings report is not nearly as important as the tariff policy, potential inflationary pressures, and even the current negotiations marked by threats of trade wars and tariffs. This is a company that makes its products in Mexico and imports those products to its automaker customers.

While the balance sheet is pristine, it might be wise to allow the political considerations to considerably abate before considering an investment. For many investors, we are likely entering a period of rough investing water in the near future.

As was noted in the article above, the timing of when economic pressures present themselves is really not certain. But for this company, things like rising prices and inflation often have an outsized effect on its auto industry customers.

At this time, this stock is likely a hold at best, and for some it would be a potential sell (as in not until there is a clear cyclical bottom).

Basically, this stock will do better when the economic is run according to the basic economics I learned a long time ago (and that begins with decreasing deficits and a potentially balanced budget. Since we do not have that right now, I do not consider this for purchasing.

However, the balance sheet is very conservative with lots of cash and almost no long-term debt. Some may want to hold this through the business cycle knowing that there are far worse investments than a company like this when the future economic picture is at best questionable. For that reason, this is a hold for this article.

Risks

The largest risks here are in economics as shown throughout the article. This is a company whose customers are very much influenced by economic policy. Therefore, a policy of declining deficits and eventually a balanced budget is highly recommended before investing in a company like this.

Other ways of running the country can definitely succeed for a time. The period from 2001-2007 demonstrated that and there are other periods as well. However, succeeding for a time is not the same as succeeding (period).

The auto industry customers are definitely influenced by economic policy, by the budget congress passes (and the president signs), and really just about anything political. Therefore, what is needed is an evaluation by the investor as to whether the current climate can prevail long-term or whether there needs to be a correction. Right now, most think that a correction (or several corrections) are needed. Hence this is probably not an investment that will succeed long-term until things change. But it does have the balance sheet to endure until those changes happen.

data.title: Did The Market Bottom Or Just Bounce?
data.description:
By Geoff Bysshe

Last week started with a steep decline in stocks that broke key support levels. This drop was a test of the bulls’ resilience and a message from the market that bad news won’t always be a buying opportunity.

Fortunately, for reasons we’ll discuss, the first day of the week was the low of the week.

As this article will explain, if last week turns out to be just a bounce followed by a break of last Tuesday’s low, there’s reason to believe a bigger correction is underway.

Geopolitics and The Economy Are Driving Precious Metals & Materials

It was easy to see why gold and silver continued their parabolic ascent last week, but did you see one of the market’s important leading sectors roll over into a warning phase, or the biggest surge in over a year in an important market internals measure?

Last week, the media was fixated on geopolitics. It was hard to ignore.

Trump’s demands that he own Greenland took center stage, but Ray Dalio also got some sensationalized attention when he released one of his stereotypical “explainer” videos from Davos in which he labelled the geopolitical atmosphere as “capital wars.”

Significantly less publicized than either of the two messages above, but infinitely more likely to become recognized as one of history’s great speeches, Mark Carney, Prime Minister of Canada, delivered a “special address” that began in French, transitioned to English, pronounced that we are in the midst of “... a rupture in the world order, the end of a pleasant fiction and the beginning of a harsh reality...”, and proposed a new world order as a solution all in about 17 minutes.

Whether you prefer to listen to President Trump, Ray Dalio, or Mark Carney, you’re likely to come to the same conclusion on one issue - It’s a good idea to own gold.

The chart below shows the best- and worst-performing sectors along with gold (GLD), silver (SLV), and the dollar (DXY).

The biggest losers included two of the market’s leading sectors - money center banks (XLF) and retail (XRT).

The banks have slipped into a warning phase, meaning they are below their 50-DMA. What happened to this group that was nicely leading the market higher? We’ll explain below.

Another big loser was the dollar.

While it's easy to jump to the stereotypical conclusion that gold is going up because the dollar is declining, the long-term perspective shown in the chart below demonstrates that gold is in a bullish trend regardless of what the dollar is doing. The correlation between the two changes dramatically over time.

Rather than stating that gold is up because the dollar is down, a better response would be to ask why both are experiencing big moves - the dollar declining and gold rallying. One answer is in both Ray Dalio’s video and Mark Carney’s speech.

Last week’s leaders were all related to either precious metals or energy.

Silver’s move wasn’t only extraordinary on its own; it also pushed its ratio to gold to a level seen in only three periods (1998-99, 2006, 2010-11) since 1990.

Stocks Have Shrugged Off Geopolitics With This Silver Lining

If fear of geopolitical uncertainty is driving gold higher, why isn’t it driving stocks lower? It is (or may be) in some areas, but it’s also driving other areas of the market higher, such as aerospace, defense, and materials.

If Mark Carney is right, the new world order will lead to more countries feeling they need to spend more to protect themselves, build more of their own supply chains, secure more raw materials for themselves, and more.

Fortunately, the global economy is growing, and in the case of the U.S., it appears to be improving. A growing economy can fuel corporate earnings that, in turn, lead to higher stock prices.

The Economy Is Quietly Supporting Stocks - For Now

Last week, there were several economic reports that suggested the labor market and inflation are stable, and growth is improving.

On Wednesday, the Atlanta Fed GDPNow report indicated that Q4 GDP is expected to be 5.4%.

As you can see from the chart above, the GDP estimates for Q4 have increased, and have remained higher since December.

On Thursday, the actual GDP for Q3 was reported to be 4.4%, which was slightly better than expectations

Also on Thursday, the inflation gauge of the Core PCE Price Index reported a value of 2.8%. While this is not the 2% level that the Fed has been targeting, it does represent inflation holding steady around 3% (see chart below), which the market has come to accept, even though experts complain about it.

Additionally, initial jobless claims were reported at 200,000, slightly better than the 209,000 forecast. More importantly, it demonstrated that the trend since early November has remained down.

So while world leaders and corporate titans were meeting at Davos, where they were generating global macro and geopolitical fireworks, the stock market was getting positive economic data.

Be Careful What You Wish For. It May Be Too Bullish?

A common refrain in market commentary for months has been a desire to see the market “broaden out.” By many measures, this has happened. For example, the equal-weighted S&P 500 (RSP) and the Russell 2000 Index (IWM) have reached new highs, while SPY and QQQ have struggled.

Remarkably, you can see in the chart below from Big View that the number of 52-week highs reached its highest level since 2024.

This can be interpreted as overbought when the trend has been accelerating, but the market has been consolidating. A confirming indicator would be for the price to move higher and the number of new highs to continue to expand. If the indexes begin to slip, this spike in new highs may look like overambitious momentum traders buying the strongest stocks after the Greenland controversy drop.

Investor enthusiasm is also running high. The chart of AAII Investor Sentiment below shows that the week before last, the % of bulls was at its highest, while the % of bears was at its lowest, resulting in the spread being the biggest since late 2024.

It’s Ok To Be Bullish, Until Something Goes Wrong

Recall that the feature image chart of this article showed that XLF was one of the worst-performing sectors last week. This is not a good sign because this had been a leading group at the end of 2025.

As you can see from the annotations on the chart below, when XLF broke above its 50-DMA in November, it had 3 of the 4 indicators in a bullish position.

When it started to break under its 10-DMA in early January, the faster Real Motion indicator had started to turn negative. By the time the price pulled back to the 50-DMA, all 4 indicators were bearish. This condition suggests that the MA can be easily broken and the price will extend lower, as it has.

** You can find the chart of above with all key indicators for all the symbols in the sectors summary table in Big View here.

Unfortunately, there is a fundamental reason for the group’s underperformance. Earnings reports have been missing expectations. In many cases, earnings growth has been strong, but analysts' estimates were too bullish.

As you can see below in the chart of FactSet’s reporting on the 10 worst earnings misses of the week, 7 of the 10 were banks.

Missing expectations and weak momentum indicators are bearish conditions that will need to be worked off for this group to regain its status as a market leader.

The bulls continue to point to the financials as a leading group. I’d fact-check them with the charts in Big View’s Sector Summary.

How Bad Is it?

Last week’s issue of Market Outlook provided a very timely look at the January Calendar Range as a guide for anticipating the markets’ trend right now and throughout the year.

This analysis was just in time for Tuesday’s gap lower in stock, which continued lower to close below the January low and the 50-DMA in both the SPY and QQQ. Tuesday’s breakdown, however, became Wednesday’s reversal, which continued in varying degrees throughout the week.

However, as we just learned, XLF didn’t bounce back. It also closed the week below its January Calendar Range low. This is bearish for the XLF and not a good sign for the market.

However, as you can see with the table below (taken from the Seasonality section of Big View), XLF is the only group below its January range. In fact, there’s only one other group that is below its January opening range (Utilities).

Furthermore, the “Stock Phase” column shows that most of the groups are in Bullish phases, which means price over the 50-DMA and the 50- over the 200-DMA.

If you’d like to learn more about the Calendar Ranges' significance and how to use them to anticipate trends and reversals, refer to last week’s Market Outlook.

Bottom or Bounce? The Calendar Ranges Will Tell

With financials - especially the big banks - typically leading earnings season, their underperformance and earnings disappointments set a cautious tone.

If the broader market follows their lead, the bounce we saw last week may turn out to be just that - a bounce, not a bottom. If XLF continues to slide, the risk of a correction increases.

However, the market’s resilience in the face of war, political drama, and rising gold suggests a deeper strength. A strong economy, steady employment, and cooling inflation could support a durable rally - provided earnings expectations weren’t set too high.

The two big levels to watch on the downside in the indexes, the sectors, and individual stocks are the January Calendar Range low that was set last Thursday and the 50-DMA.

On the upside, follow the leaders.

Best wishes for your trading.

As promised, for a deeper perspective on the tectonic shifts in geopolitics and capital flows presented by Ray Dalio’s video and Mark Carney's Special Address at Davos.

  • Ray Dalio’s “Capital Wars” explainer video from Davos. The title is sensationalized by the media to get clicks, but it's also a good description of what’s been growing in intensity since the start of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. The multi-year bull market in gold is one example of how influential these wars that most people don’t see can be.
  • Mark Carney’s historic “rupture” speech - A clear description of what’s wrong with international relations, and a call for a new world order to fix it.

Big View Summary

By Keith Schneider

Every week, we review the big picture of the market's technical condition as seen through the lens of our Big View data charts.

The bullets provide a quick summary organized by conditions we see as being risk-on, risk-off, or neutral.

Summary: Overall conditions remain risk-on as major indexes hold bullish phases, market internals and new-high/new-low trends are strong, volatility is subdued, global markets are leading, and seasonal trends stay supportive into mid-February. However, risk-off undercurrents are rising with weak volume patterns, ongoing growth underperformance, and a sharp surge in gold, silver, oil, and a breakdown in the dollar - suggesting growing defensive behavior and macro or geopolitical stress beneath the surface.

Risk-On

  • Indexes are flat or mixed on the week, with all of them maintaining their bullish phase for daily and weekly charts. (+)
  • Market internals deteriorated slightly this week but remain mostly risk-on. The cumulative advance-decline line hit new highs this week. An early warning that things may be shifting is if the up/down volume ratio falls below 50%. (+)
  • The moving averages of the 52-week new high new low ratio improved and is in a strong condition for both S&P and Nasdaq. (+)
  • The color charts (moving average of stocks above key moving averages) is positive for the Russell and S&P across the board and more neutral for the Nasdaq. (+)
  • Volatility remains low after the spike early in the week. (+)
  • Modern Family is still risk-on, with all of them in bull phases and good relative leadership with positive volume patterns. (+)
  • Foreign equities continue to lead the U.S., up strong on the week and none of them are overbought. (+)
  • The SPY and QQQ are lagging their seasonal trends, with the exception of IWM. The seasonal trends remain generally positive through mid-February. (+)

Neutral

  • Volume patterns remain weak with more distribution days than accumulation days in all the indexes except IWM. Overall, a weak neutral. (=)
  • U.S. sector performance this week was mixed, with semiconductors strong but financials and transports weak. (=)
  • Growth stocks continue to lag value since the November/December time frame. Value is in a strong bull phase, while growth is in a strong warning phase. (=)
  • DBA appears to be coming out of its base and is now showing short-term leadership versus the S&P. (=)
  • Interest rates continue to trade in a tight range. (=)

Risk-Off

  • Gold continues its parabolic move to new highs, along with silver. (-)
  • With commodities, led by silver, and with the dollar getting hit hard along with retail, we see more of a risk-off signal from the five-day biggest leaders. (-)
  • Risk gauges, with the strength in gold, are strongly risk-off, with the SPY/XLU ratio the only one showing positive. (-)
  • Oil is now trading above its 200-Day Moving Average, and with the parabolic move in the metals, it could be indicating more expected geopolitical stress. (-)
  • The dollar broke down hard this week to its lowest levels since September. (-)

Actionable Trading Plan

  1. Core posture - Stay invested, but tighten discipline. Maintain core long exposure while markets remain in bullish phases and internals/new-high data stay constructive, but avoid adding broad index risk aggressively until volume patterns improve and risk gauges stabilize.
  2. Tilt allocations, don’t go all-in. Favor areas still showing leadership and healthier participation (foreign equities, selected Modern Family leaders, semiconductors, IWM-style breadth) while underweight lagging segments (growth vs. value, weak U.S. sectors, retail-linked names).
  3. Add a defined defensive sleeve. Given the strong signals from metals, commodities, and the dollar breakdown, maintain or initiate measured exposure to gold/silver/commodity strength and consider them both return drivers and portfolio shock absorbers.
  4. Shorten time frames and tighten risk control. Trade smaller, use quicker profit-taking, and reduce tolerance for drawdowns. Any new positions should prove themselves quickly or be cut.
  5. Use internals and volume as the trigger, not price levels. If up/down volume weakens further, distribution days expand, or risk gauges stay firmly risk-off, begin systematically scaling down equity exposure and rotating further toward defensive/real-asset leadership.
  6. Scenario planning.
    • If internals re-strengthen and volume improves → gradually re-expand offensive exposure.
    • If metals, oil, and the dollar trend persist with weakening participation → shift toward capital preservation mode and treat equity longs as tactical, not core.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: BUI: Reasons For Optimism, But The Current Price Is Too High
data.description:
The BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure & Power Opportunities Trust (BUI) is a closed-end fund, or CEF, that invests in the utilities sector or other types of infrastructure, such as midstream energy. As is the case with most closed-end funds, BUI has a very attractive distribution yield relative to many other things in the market.

However, as today’s yield is only 6.14%, there are several closed-end funds with higher yields. This is an equity fund, which is nice, as equities typically do a better job of preserving an investor’s purchasing power over time than any type of fixed-income security. In addition, utilities and infrastructure companies both tend to have business models that allow them to increase their revenues at least as quickly as inflation, which adds a degree of protection to investors.

As most regular readers are well aware, one of my biggest concerns in the coming years is that inflation will run much higher than we have all become accustomed to over the past several decades, which could cause a retiree’s assets to run out more rapidly than they expected unless they have sufficient exposure to assets that provide protection against the loss of purchasing power that will accompany inflation. The BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust could be one such asset, and the fact that it provides a high yield is a bonus.

The BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust Versus Equity Indices

As was stated in the introduction, the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust is an equity closed-end fund that invests in the common stock of utility companies, electric producers, and infrastructure companies (including midstream energy). The company’s third-quarter 2025 holdings report states that BUI had the following asset allocation on September 30, 2025:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets

Common Stocks

98.0%

Money Market Funds

2.4%

Options Written

-0.9%

As we can see, the fund’s entire portfolio consists solely of common equities except for a small allocation to cash and some short call options positions that the fund wrote against the positions in its portfolio. The short call option positions are due to the fund’s covered call-writing strategy, which allows it to increase the income that it earns from its positions (from the premiums that it is paid upfront for the sale) and eliminates the fund’s need to employ leverage to boost the effective yields that it can earn from the assets in its portfolio. This could be a good thing, depending on how we look at it. On the one hand, leverage increases both gains and losses, so it increases the volatility of the share price.

Many income investors are retirees or other individuals who would prefer not to have the value of their assets swing wildly from day to day, and the fact that this fund avoids leverage reduces the risk that this will happen with its share price. However, on the other hand, the use of options reduces the amount of capital gains that the fund can earn from any given position. This can lower the total returns that the fund is able to provide to its investors, and ultimately result in the fund’s shareholders having less wealth over the long term than if it were not using the covered call-writing strategy. The fund is basically sacrificing capital growth for income, and it is up to each individual to make their own decision about whether or not this is acceptable.

As the fund’s portfolio consists almost entirely of common equities, it is most appropriate to compare it with the major common equity indices. As we can see here, the fund has a substantially higher yield than any broad-market equity index:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

S&P 500 Index (SPY)

1.06%

NASDAQ-100 Index (QQQ)

0.45%

Russell 2000 Index (IWM)

0.96%

As of the time of writing, the highest-yielding of the three American broad-market equity indices shown is the S&P 500 Index. This may be surprising to some readers who have been following my work for a long time, as the Russell 2000 had a higher yield than the other two over most of the past few years. This was, at least partially, due to size. The S&P 500 Index (SP500) and the NASDAQ-100 Index (NDX) are both primarily large-capitalization indices. The S&P 500 Index comprises the largest companies by market capitalization in the United States, whereas the NASDAQ-100 Index comprises the 100 largest companies by market capitalization trading on the NASDAQ Stock Exchange that are not in the financial sector.

Thus, both of these indices are large-capitalization indices by design. The Russell 2000 is not, as it is the 2000 smallest companies in the broader Russell 3000 Index. Over nearly all of the past decade, large-capitalization companies have outperformed smaller companies. We can see this quite clearly by looking at the trailing ten-year price performance of all three of the above indices:

As is immediately shown, the Russell 2000 Index lagged the other two by a significant margin over the period. We can debate whether or not this was justified, as the Russell 2000 Index includes many companies with fairly weak balance sheets, along with zombie companies (companies that only generate enough revenue to cover their expenses and service debt), that dragged on its performance when compared to the other two indices that have a higher proportion of companies that actually have the ability to finance their growth. This resulted in the S&P 500 Index and the NASDAQ-100 having higher valuations than the Russell 2000:

Index

Current P/E Ratio

S&P 500

27.60

NASDAQ-100

33.33

Russell 2000

18.57

Stock valuations can be supported by the expectations that investors have surrounding the forward growth of the company that issued the stock, even if they are unrealistic. However, dividends must be paid by actual cash that a company generates. As such, there is an inverse correlation between valuation and yield. The fact that the S&P 500 Index and the NASDAQ-100 had higher valuations than the Russell 2000 resulted in those indices having lower yields than the Russell 2000 for most of the past few years. This has changed, however, such that the S&P 500 Index now has a higher yield than the other two indices.

With that said, though, none of these indices are particularly attractive in terms of yield. As just shown, the largest exchange-traded index fund tracking the S&P 500 Index as of the time of writing only has a 1.06% yield at its current price. That yield means that someone who invests $1 million into the index fund will only earn $10,600 per year in annual income. That is lower than the amount of money that would be earned from a minimum-wage job at full-time hours. Clearly, the broad-market equity indices are not paying attractive yields right now. The BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust is easily beating all of them in terms of yield.

At this point, some readers might point out that comparing BUI’s yield to that of the broad-market indices is not entirely appropriate. The name of the fund suggests that the fund invests primarily in utilities and infrastructure companies, and indeed, the fund’s portfolio agrees with that statement. The fund’s website states that it had the following portfolio on December 31, 2025:

As shown, the fund had about 53.29% of its assets invested in utilities and another 13.85% of its assets invested in energy infrastructure companies. Both of these sectors have much higher yields than the technology companies that tend to dominate the broad market domestic indices. It, therefore, may be more appropriate to compare this fund against the sector indices to get a better idea of how well it truly compares. Here is a chart that performs this comparison:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

The Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU)

2.72%

The Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE)

2.98%

Alerian Energy Infrastructure ETF (ENFR)

4.54%

All three of these are exchange-traded index funds tracking an index of common equities in their respective sectors. We can see that the 6.14% yield of the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust manages to surpass all of them. However, in this case, the index funds do provide investors in them with a higher level of income. However, with the exception of the Alerian Energy Infrastructure ETF, the yields are still lower than what a typical money market fund would pay out right now.

We should note, however, that there are some energy infrastructure exchange-traded index funds that deliver higher yields than BUI. In particular, the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) and Global X MLP ETF (MLPA) both have yields well above 7%. However, the two indices that these ETFs track consist exclusively of master limited partnerships, which are very different than the C-corporations that the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust invests in. As such, neither of those two indices is directly comparable, but they might be interesting for someone who wishes to invest in energy infrastructure and wants to earn a high level of income at the same time.

The BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust has just over half of its assets invested in utilities right now, but it has delivered substantially higher total returns over the past decade than the Utilities Select Sector Index. In fact, out of the above three most comparable common stock indices, only the Alerian Energy Infrastructure ETF has managed to beat BUI over the past ten years:

However, the difference between BUI and the ENFR index fund was not very great. Over the entire ten-year period, BUI only underperformed by 14.45%. That is not a huge difference considering that it is the cumulative amount of difference over a ten-year period (not 14.45% per year). In addition to this, we can see in the chart above that the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust was less volatile than ENFR. The energy infrastructure ETF was affected much more than BUI by the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020 and the decline in oil prices that accompanied it.

Due to that large decline, ENFR was actually underperforming BUI until mid-2023. As was already stated, many income investors are retirees or other individuals who do not want large variations in the value of their portfolios over time. When combined with the higher relative yield of BUI versus ENFR, this consideration might make BUI more desirable than the index fund despite the slightly lower yield.

We should also note that the above performance analysis made the assumption that investors reinvested all of the distributions that each of the funds paid out into additional shares of the respective funds. This may not be a reasonable assumption since income investors will frequently prefer to take the distributions paid out by the funds as spendable income. For someone who did this over the period, BUI saw the worst price appreciation over the period:

Over the past ten years, shares of BUI only gained 57.78%. Clearly, this is lower than the other funds delivered over the same period. However, the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust did have lower volatility than either of the two energy index funds. This was at least somewhat expected, given that a covered call-writing strategy can help to reduce portfolio volatility because such a strategy reduces the potential capital gains as well as increases income (and thus partially offsetting declines in equity prices).

Ultimately, the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities seems to compare pretty well with the most comparable index funds, depending on the exact goals that an investor has.

Comparing The BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust With Its Peers

As we just saw, the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust has a substantially higher yield than any of the major equity indices, including those tracking similar assets to what this particular fund holds. This is not particularly surprising as BUI is a closed-end fund, and as a closed-end fund it has the ability to employ certain tactics to boost its yield that are unavailable to an index fund. For example, BUI engages in the writing of covered call options to produce premium income and increase the effective yield that the fund earns on its net assets.

In addition, the fund is actively managed and may realize capital gains on the assets that it holds. The fund’s most recent semi-annual report states that it had a 27% turnover ratio for the six-month period that ended on June 30, 2025, so it is definitely realizing capital gains from time to time. The fund can pay out these realized capital gains to its shareholders, resulting in the fund’s yield being higher than would be possible through income alone.

While equity index funds do not really make much use of either of these strategies, the closed-end fund peers of the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust may be able to do so. This allows them to be more competitive with it in terms of yield. We certainly see that when we compare BUI’s yield to that of other closed-end funds:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust

Equity-Sector Equity

6.14%

Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF)

Equity-Sector Equity

7.38%

DNP Select Income Fund (DNP)

Equity-Sector Equity

7.77%

Duff & Phelps Utility and Infrastructure Fund (DPG)

Equity-Sector Equity

6.41%

Gabelli Utility Trust (GUT)

Equity-Sector Equity

9.79%

Reaves Utility Income Fund (UTG)

Equity-Sector Equity

6.35%

Arguably, the funds that are closest to BUI in composition are the Duff & Phelps Utility and Infrastructure Fund and the Reaves Utility Income Fund. DPG, in particular, consists of a combination of utility and midstream energy stocks, just like the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust:

The Reaves Utility Income Fund is even more heavily invested in utility companies, as its largest positions are almost entirely comprised of utility companies:

The Reaves Utility Income Fund is a focused fund with 55.86% of its assets invested in the top ten companies, along with a whopping 11.49% of its total assets invested in Talen Energy (TLN). The BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust is somewhat more diversified by comparison as its largest position, NextEra Energy (NEE), only accounts for 6.93% of the fund’s total assets:

We can see that all ten of the largest positions in BUI’s portfolio only account for 39.46% of the fund’s total assets, considerably less than UTG has invested in its ten largest positions. In addition, BUI includes midstream companies such as The Williams Companies (WMB), TC Energy (TRP), and Cheniere Energy (LNG) among its largest positions, which adds some diversity that UTG lacks. This could be attractive to investors who wish to have exposure to both utilities and energy infrastructure, although an individual investor could also gain exposure to both types of companies by simply buying a pure utility fund, such as UTG, along with an energy infrastructure fund.

We can see that the yields of BUI, DPG, and UTG are comparable, with BUI’s being slightly lower than the other two. The Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund could be argued to be reasonably close in terms of yield, but that fund’s portfolio composition is also quite a bit different from the other two as it also includes exposure to freight railroads, cellular towers, airports, toll roads, and other types of infrastructure. While UTF does have 42.24% of its assets invested in electric and natural gas utilities as of the time of writing, it is a very different fund, and investors who want to profit from utility companies are unlikely to consider that particular fund.

The BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust had by far the best performance out of the three similar-yielding utility funds over the past ten years:

The fund also outperformed the Gabelli Utility Trust over the same period on a total return basis, despite that fund’s significantly higher yield. One possible reason for this is that BUI has higher energy midstream exposure than the other two funds, and that sector did very well against utilities over the ten-year period. We saw this earlier in this article with ENFR significantly outperforming XLU.

However, we should note that the Reaves Utility Income Fund has a better history of distribution growth over time than BUI does. As shown here, UTG has consistently increased its distribution since its inception:

While BUI has also grown its distribution over time and has been very reliable, it does not deliver consistent distribution growth. Rather, its distribution has historically been very stable with only a very small increase after many years. BUI’s monthly distribution was flat from 2014 through late 2024, for example. One goal that income investors should have, especially if they are depending on their portfolio to provide them with the income that they need to support themselves in retirement, is to have their income grow over time. This is needed because otherwise inflation will be steadily reducing the purchasing power of their incomes. UTG has been somewhat better at satisfying this requirement than BUI over time.

Given that BUI has delivered much stronger total returns, history suggests that the best option for investors needing income growth is to use some of BUI’s distributions to purchase additional shares of the fund. However, that does reduce the spendable income that an investor receives today.

Recent Performance And Thesis Update

We last discussed the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust on November 27, 2024. As such, approximately fourteen months have passed since we last looked at this fund. As everyone reading this is no doubt well aware, there are a lot of things that can occur during a given fourteen-month period. The most recent one in certainly no exception to this, as the utility sector in particular has been at the center of a major trend occurring in the market.

The major trend to which I am referring is, of course, the emergence of generative artificial intelligence as an economic force and the need for data centers to satisfy this demand. According to Reuters, the combined computing capacity of all data centers in the United States will increase by 1,000% if all of the data centers currently being planned or constructed ultimately end up being finished and brought online:

We can see that, while Texas and Virginia are currently seeing the most activity among data center operators, very few states are actually being left out of their plans. It makes sense that a massive number of data centers are being planned, as generative artificial intelligence technology is very processing-intensive and requires specially designed hardware to even function properly. Goldman Sachs believes that generative artificial intelligence will replace approximately 300 million jobs by 2030, with companies using the technology to do approximately 25% of the workload currently being done by humans.

Personally, I suspect that this estimate will prove wildly optimistic given that artificial intelligence cannot be trained on data sets produced by artificial intelligence (which means that it cannot fully replace humans in most tasks), cannot actually produce creative output (it can only derive output from human-produced input and gets very repetitive with its outputs over time), and almost every company that has attempted to use artificial intelligence to automate its processes says that the investment was not worth the money. Nevertheless, we can see that the technology sector is planning to construct massive numbers of new data centers that are intended to support the optimistic goals that investors and technology executives currently have.

One characteristic of the specialized hardware required to make generative artificial intelligence work is that it requires enormous amounts of electricity. According to the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, a simple task done with a generative artificial intelligence chatbot uses a phenomenal amount of electricity. The university’s researchers state that asking the chatbot fifteen questions, producing ten images, along with a five-second-long video uses about 2.9 kilowatt-hours of electricity. That is enough electricity to power an electric car for ten miles (or an electric bike for 100 miles), or enough electricity to run a typical residential microwave for more than thirty minutes. The task just used as an example is not a particularly intense one, either, as anyone trying to organize a charity dinner or find product recommendations would almost certainly do at least as much as the three tasks mentioned. When we combine this with the number of people in the population that researchers believe will end up using generative artificial intelligence technology, we can easily see how the construction of data centers and the expanded use of artificial intelligence will result in a massive increase in electricity consumption in the United States.

Reuters quantifies this potential growth in electricity consumption in the article that I linked to in a previous paragraph. As of today, all of the data centers in the United States combined require just under 15 gigawatts of electricity to power all of the computing equipment contained within their walls. The total amount of power that will be needed to power all of the data centers for which plans have already been filed with the authorities is 150 gigawatts, or roughly ten times the amount of power currently consumed by all of the data centers operating in the United States. Admittedly, it is not certain that all of the planned data centers will ultimately be constructed or brought to operation, and it is almost certain that not all of them will ultimately be consummated if the demand for generative artificial intelligence fails to live up to the current hopes of many people. In addition, those entities working to construct new data center capacity have struggled to get regulatory approval in all areas, and indeed, given the struggles of electric utilities to provide enough reliable electricity in certain areas of the country, it is very possible that they will not be able to get approval to construct all of the data centers that are currently being planned. However, it is still a fair assumption that the construction of new data centers to provide the infrastructure needed for generative artificial intelligence will cause an increase in the amount of electricity that is consumed in the United States, and this might be a very large increase in consumption.

This is the bull case for utilities that has emerged since the last time that we discussed the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust. An electric utility’s revenue is directly proportional to the amount of electricity that its customers consume during a given period. As the planned data centers are completed and come online, they will consume electricity and naturally boost the revenues of electric utilities. Electric utilities are not likely to be the only beneficiaries of this trend. Due to the difficulties that data center owners have been having securing a reliable source of electricity in certain areas, some data centers have on-site power generation in which they use a natural gas turbine to produce electricity to power the data centers. The natural gas to fuel these turbines can come from either a natural gas utility or a midstream company that owns natural gas pipelines.

The BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust invests in both electric and natural gas utilities, and it also invests in midstream companies. Thus, several of the fund’s investments could be poised to benefit from data center and generative artificial intelligence expansion. The market is forward-looking, so it is very possible that we have already seen the potential revenue increase for utilities and midstream companies priced into BUI’s share price.

The share price of the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust is up by 11.73% since the last time that we discussed the fund:

As we can see, the fund substantially outperformed the domestic utilities index, and it did a reasonable job at keeping pace with the S&P 500 Index. This is very surprising considering the assets that this fund holds, as neither utilities nor midstream stocks kept up with the S&P 500 Index over that period. Indeed, both lagged, as utilities were only up 2.68% while ENFR was only up 1.04% over the same period.

The fund’s portfolio performance was more in line with the utilities and Alerian Energy Infrastructure indices than it was with the S&P 500 Index:

This chart shows the performance of the fund’s net asset value since our last discussion on this fund. As we can clearly see, the fund’s portfolio only increased by 3.99% on a per-share basis. Thus, the fund’s share price performance vastly exceeded the performance of the actual portfolio. This points to the fund being significantly more expensive today than it was fourteen months ago.

On a total return basis, the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust substantially outperformed any of the indices:

We can see that investors who reinvested the distributions that the fund paid out over the period have seen the size of their position increase by 25.47% in fourteen months. The same figure for the S&P 500 (SP500TR) is only 17.00%, and both the utilities and energy infrastructure indices did worse. While this is certainly impressive, it is important that we keep in mind that the fund’s portfolio did not do this well. Over the period, the fund only covered its distributions plus 3.99%, which is not anywhere near 25.47%. As such, we should pay attention to the fund’s valuation before buying in, as there is a risk that we will overpay if we purchase the fund at today’s price.

Distribution Analysis

The website for the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust states that the fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a high level of total return and current income. As is the case with most closed-end funds, BUI primarily provides its returns through the payment of a monthly distribution to its shareholders. The monthly distribution amount is $0.1363 per share ($1.6356 per share annually), which gives the fund a 6.14% yield at the current price.

Let us have a look at the fund’s finances to see how well the fund is covering its distribution. For this purpose, we will use the fund’s semi-annual report for the six-month period that ended on June 30, 2025. Admittedly, this report is several months old as of the time of writing, but it is the newest financial report available right now, and it is newer than the report that was available the last time that we discussed this fund. As such, it should work reasonably well to provide us with an update.

For the six-month period that ended on June 30, 2025, the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust reported a net investment income of $3,304,985. This was not sufficient to cover the $18,491,348 that the fund paid out in distributions over the same time period.

Fortunately, this fund was able to make up for the difference through capital gains. For the six-month period that ended on June 30, 2025, BUI reported net realized gains of $16,185,630 and an additional $51,246,675 in net unrealized gains. The fund’s investment portfolio overall generated $70,737,290 in total returns.

The fund’s net investment income and net realized gains together were enough to cover the distribution during the first six months of 2025. While we do not have a financial report for the second half of 2025 yet, the fact that the fund paid out a special distribution of $1.0383 per share for the 2025 fiscal year strongly suggests that this fund fully covered its distributions over the past twelve months. For the most part, it does not appear that investors in this fund need to be concerned about its ability to sustain the distribution.

Valuation

The BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure & Power Opportunities Trust currently trades at a 5.73% premium to net asset value. This is a larger premium than the 4.60% that the fund’s shares have traded at on average over the past month.

Over the past five years, BUI has traded at prices ranging from an 8.26% discount on net asset value to an 11.47% premium on net asset value. The average price over the period was a 0.09% premium, so this fund is currently trading at a price that is far above its historical average.

Here is how the current valuation of the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust compares to its peers:

We immediately see that a number of the peer funds trade at premiums on net asset value, which could reflect investor optimism on the ability of utilities to produce growth as the artificial intelligence thesis plays out. However, it rarely makes sense to buy any fund at a premium to net asset value, as such a scenario basically means that it should be possible to purchase the underlying stocks in the portfolio for cheaper than the fund’s shares. When we combine this with the fact that BUI is trading at a price that is higher than its long-term average, it becomes difficult to make a case for buying this fund today. However, should the price of the fund’s shares decline to a point at which they are closer to its net asset value, then purchasing it begins to make sense.

Expenses

The semi-annual report states that BUI had an annualized expense ratio of 1.11% for the six-month period that ended on June 30, 2025.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure, & Power Opportunities Trust is a fund that invests primarily in utilities and midstream energy companies in order to provide investors with a respectable 6.14% yield. The fund has a very strong performance history, but it has recently begun to look overvalued as the shares have substantially outperformed the portfolio itself, and anyone purchasing the shares of this fund at today’s levels is paying more for the underlying assets than they are actually worth.

While there could be reasons to be optimistic about the growth opportunities possessed by some of the companies in the BUI fund’s portfolio, it is hard to justify paying the current price. This fund could be worth considering if its shares fall into line with the actual portfolio, however.
